title: Advanced Array Methods
tagline: Transform data like a pro with map, reduce, and filter.

accomplishments: Transform an array of numbers into an array of their doubles
Convert an array of products into an array of formatted prices
Build a temperature converter that transforms entire datasets

do.title: Double Every Number
steps: Click each button and watch the transformations!
do.code:
🔄 The Map Method

Original Array:
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Transformed Array:
Click a button to transform
do.result: .map() took every number in the array and transformed it according to the function you provided!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the squared numbers result [1, 4, 9, 16, 25].

concepts.name: What is .map()?
concepts.definition: Transforms every item in an array and returns a NEW array with the transformed values. The original array stays unchanged. array.map(function(item) { return transformed; })
concepts.analogy: An assembly line. Each item goes in, gets transformed, comes out changed. The original raw materials are still there — you get a new collection of finished products.

concepts.name: .map() returns a NEW array
concepts.definition: Unlike .push() or .pop(), .map() doesn't modify the original array. It creates a brand new array. You must store the result: let doubled = numbers.map(...).
concepts.analogy: Taking a photo of a painting — the original painting is unchanged, you just have a new copy to work with.

concepts.name: Arrow function syntax
concepts.definition: => replaces the word function for cleaner code. numbers.map(num => num * 2) is the same as numbers.map(function(num) { return num * 2; }). Single-line arrow functions auto-return.
concepts.analogy: Shorthand — like writing "btw" instead of "by the way." Same meaning, less typing.

concepts.name: Extracting properties from objects
concepts.definition: Common pattern: users.map(user => user.name) extracts just the names from an array of user objects. Great for pulling specific data out of complex arrays.
concepts.analogy: Going through a stack of business cards and copying just the phone numbers onto a new list.

concepts.name: Chaining array methods
concepts.definition: Because .map() returns a new array, you can chain it with other methods: input.split('\n').map(parseFloat).filter(n => !isNaN(n)). Each step transforms the result of the previous.
concepts.analogy: A factory assembly line with multiple stations — cut, paint, polish, package. Each step passes its output to the next.

understand.keyTakeaway: Use .map() when you need to transform every item in an array. It creates a new array, leaves the original alone, and makes code cleaner than for loops.

build.goal: Build a temperature converter that transforms arrays of temperatures between Celsius and Fahrenheit
build.hint: Formula for C to F: (C * 9/5) + 32. Formula for F to C: (F - 32) * 5/9. Use .toFixed(1) to round to 1 decimal place.

challenge.title: Build a Price Formatter
challenge.mission: Create a product price formatter that applies different transformations (add tax, apply discount, convert currency) using .map().
requirements: Array of at least 5 products (name, price)
"Convert to EUR" button multiplies prices by 0.92
Display original and transformed prices side-by-side
challenge.starterCode: 💰 Price Formatter
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a "Reset" button that shows original prices, and a combo button that applies discount THEN adds tax!

capabilities: Use .map() to transform every item in an array
Understand that .map() creates a new array
Write arrow functions for cleaner code
Extract properties from arrays of objects

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn .forEach() for side effects, .reduce() for aggregating data, and when to use each array method.

accomplishments: Write concise arrow functions instead of long function syntax
Pass functions as arguments to other functions
Chain array methods to process data elegantly

do.title: Transform Data with Array Methods
steps: Click each button to see different transformations
Notice how much cleaner this is than loops!
do.code:
🚀 Modern Array Methods

📊 Original Data:

[
  { name: "Laptop", price: 999, category: "electronics" },
  { name: "Phone", price: 699, category: "electronics" },
  { name: "Desk", price: 299, category: "furniture" },
  { name: "Chair", price: 199, category: "furniture" },
  { name: "Monitor", price: 399, category: "electronics" }
]

1. .map() - Transform each item

2. .filter() - Select items that match

3. .reduce() - Combine into single value

4. Method Chaining
do.result: You transformed arrays with modern methods instead of loops. Much cleaner and more readable!

concepts.name: Arrow Functions Syntax
concepts.definition: A shorter way to write functions. const add = (a, b) => a + b replaces function add(a, b) { return a + b; }. Single parameter needs no parentheses: num => num * 2.
concepts.analogy: Shorthand like "btw" instead of "by the way" — same meaning, way less typing.

concepts.name: Callbacks
concepts.definition: A function passed as an argument to another function. Array methods like .map(), .filter(), and .reduce() all accept callback functions that determine what they do.
concepts.analogy: Telling someone "when you get there, do this" — you hand them instructions to follow later.

concepts.name: .map() - transform each item
concepts.definition: Creates a NEW array by calling a function on every element. array.map(item => transformed). Always returns an array of the same length as the original.
concepts.analogy: An assembly line — every item comes in, gets transformed, comes out changed.

concepts.name: .filter() - select items
concepts.definition: Creates a NEW array with only items that pass a test (return true). array.filter(item => condition). Can return a shorter or empty array.
concepts.analogy: A bouncer at a club — only lets in people who meet the criteria, sends the rest away.

concepts.name: .reduce() - combine to one value
concepts.definition: Reduces an array to a SINGLE value. array.reduce((accumulator, item) => newAccumulator, initialValue). Great for sums, averages, counting, flattening.
concepts.analogy: Squeezing a sponge — takes in a whole array and wrings it out into one result.

concepts.name: Method chaining
concepts.definition: Because .map() and .filter() return arrays, you can chain them: products.filter(p => p.category === "electronics").map(p => p.price).reduce((sum, p) => sum + p, 0). Each line is one transformation step.
concepts.analogy: An assembly line with multiple stations — each station passes its output to the next.

understand.keyTakeaway: Arrow functions are concise. Array methods (.map, .filter, .reduce) replace most loops. Chain them together for powerful data transformations.

build.goal: Build a product catalog with category and price filters using method chaining
build.hint: Use .map() to create HTML for each product, then .join('') to combine into one string for innerHTML.

challenge.title: Build a Student Grade Analyzer
challenge.mission: Create a grade analyzer that processes student data using ONLY arrow functions and array methods (no for loops!).
requirements: Array of student objects with: name, scores (array of test scores), attendance (%)
Calculate average score for each student using .reduce()
Use .map() to add grade property (A/B/C/D/F based on average)
Use .filter() to find students with average < 60 (need help)
Sort and slice to get top 3 students
Find highest and lowest scores using Math.max/min with spread
All calculations must use arrow functions and array methods (NO for loops!)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a search feature that filters students by name using .filter() and arrow functions!

bridge.preview: In the final lesson of this module, you'll learn how to ORGANIZE large codebases — breaking code into modules, following best practices, and building maintainable applications.
